feynman.0: Gravity is the first field we get to treat with real numbers — how strong it is, what force it pushes with, how much energy you bank by climbing through it, and the orbits it holds together without any engine running at all.
feynman.1: Every mass has a gravitational field wrapped around it. The gravitational field strength g at some point is just the gravitational pull felt by each kilogram of mass you set down there. For a round body of mass M, the field strength a distance r from its centre comes out as:
feynman.2: Field strength is a vector — it has a direction. Everywhere it points straight at the centre of the source mass, so a planet’s field spreads out radially like spokes. And the size of it follows the inverse-square law: go twice as far from the centre and the field drops to a quarter.
feynman.3: Let’s put in the formula-sheet numbers for Earth (M = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg, r = RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m) and see what falls out: g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.37 × 10⁶)² = 9.81 N kg⁻¹ right at the surface. Move out to r = 2RE from the centre and the field is 9.81 ÷ 4 = 2.45 N kg⁻¹; out at 3RE it’s 9.81 ÷ 9 = 1.09 N kg⁻¹. Down inside the planet the formula quits on you: the field tails off toward zero at the very centre, so the graph of g against r has a sharp kink right at the surface, like Figure 2.1 shows.
feynman.4: Those two units for field strength are really the same thing: 1 N kg⁻¹ = 1 m s⁻². The force the field puts on a mass m is Fg = mg, so something falling freely picks up acceleration a = Fg/m = g. Notice the mass cancels right out — every object in free fall at the same spot speeds up at exactly the same rate, no matter how heavy it is.
feynman.5: Key idea Gravitational field strength at a point is the force per kilogram — which is the same as the free-fall acceleration of anything you drop there. N kg⁻¹ and m s⁻² are just two names for one unit.
feynman.6: Connects Every projectile and vertical-circle problem back in AOS 1 leans on this very field: that steady downward 9.8 m s⁻² near the ground is just g = GM/r² pretending to be uniform — which it nearly is, because the heights you deal with are tiny next to RE.
feynman.7: Any two masses pull on each other. Newton’s law of universal gravitation tells you how big the force is that each one feels from the other:
feynman.8: These two forces are a Newton’s third law pair: the pull Earth puts on the satellite and the pull the satellite puts back on Earth are equal in size and opposite in direction, as Figure 2.2 shows. They never cancel, and here’s why — they act on different objects. The universal law and the field picture tell the same story: the force on a mass m where the field strength is g is Fg = mg, because g = GM/r².
feynman.9: Trap A satellite “at an altitude of 630 km” is not sitting at r = 6.30 × 10⁵ m. Altitude h is measured up from the surface, but r is measured from Earth’s centre: r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m. Forgetting to add RE — or throwing an altitude in km in with a radius in m — is the single most common slip in this whole topic.
feynman.10: Lucia is tracking a 750 kg communications satellite going round in a circle at an altitude of 6.30 × 10⁵ m. She treats the satellite as a point mass and reaches for the formula-sheet data for Earth.
feynman.11: Formula: Fg = G m1m2r² with r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m.
feynman.13: Answer: Fg = 6.09 × 10³ N, pointing from the satellite in toward Earth’s centre. And by Newton’s third law the satellite tugs back on Earth with a force of 6.09 × 10³ N, pointing from Earth’s centre out toward the satellite.
feynman.14: Lift a mass up through a gravitational field and you bank gravitational potential energy. Near the surface, where g barely budges from 9.81 N kg⁻¹, the change is simply:
feynman.15: For height changes that are small next to RE — a thrown ball, a lift, an aircraft — this uniform-field shortcut works beautifully. But for a satellite hauled up through thousands of kilometres it breaks down, because g itself is fading as you climb. The trick that always works swaps mgΔh for an area.
feynman.16: The change in gravitational potential energy is just the area under a force–distance graph between the two distances — the same work-is-area idea you already used for springs and trolleys back in earlier units. If instead the graph plots field strength g against distance, then its area carries units of (N kg⁻¹) × m = J kg⁻¹: it’s telling you the energy change per kilogram, so you multiply by the mass m to get ΔEg. The lovely part is that a field–distance graph doesn’t care what’s orbiting, so one graph does the job for satellites of every mass.
feynman.17: To get an area, you count grid squares. First work out what one square is worth — the force its height spans times the distance its width spans — then count every square that’s at least half buried under the curve. In Figure 2.3 each square is worth 500 N × 5.0 × 10⁵ m = 2.5 × 10⁸ J, and about 12.5 squares sit under the curve between r = 7.0 × 10⁶ m and r = 9.0 × 10⁶ m, so ΔEg ≈ 12.5 × 2.5 × 10⁸ ≈ 3.1 × 10⁹ J. Always check the axis units before you multiply: distances love to show up in km, and the axes often hide a scale factor like ×10⁶.
feynman.18: A satellite in a steady circular orbit has exactly one force on it: the gravitational pull from the central body. Nothing else is balancing it — nothing. The satellite is forever accelerating toward the centre while its sideways velocity carries it around instead of letting it fall straight down. It moves at constant speed but not constant velocity, because its direction is changing every single instant.
feynman.19: The gravitational field hands over precisely the centripetal acceleration that uniform circular motion demands. For a circular orbit of radius r and period T:
feynman.20: Set the field strength equal to the centripetal acceleration and the orbit gets tied to the central mass: GM/r² = v²/r = 4π²r/T². Look where the satellite’s own mass shows up — nowhere. For a given central body, once you pick the orbital radius you’ve fixed the speed and the period: a stray bolt and a space station at the same radius orbit in lockstep.
feynman.21: Key idea Orbital speed, period and acceleration hang only on the central mass M and the orbital radius r — never on the mass of the satellite.
feynman.22: A geostationary satellite hangs over the same point on the equator forever, so receiving dishes never have to chase it across the sky. Kenji models its motion as a uniform circular orbit in the plane of the equator, going round the way Earth spins with a period of one day, T = 24 h = 8.64 × 10⁴ s.
feynman.23: Formula: the field supplies the centripetal acceleration, G Mr² = 4π²rT², which you rearrange into r³ = GMT²4π².
feynman.25: Evaluation: r = ∛(7.53 × 10²²) = 4.22 × 10⁷ m, so the altitude works out to h = r − RE = 4.22 × 10⁷ − 6.37 × 10⁶ = 3.59 × 10⁷ m (roughly 36 000 km up).
feynman.26: Answer: the orbital speed is v = 2πr/T = (2π × 4.22 × 10⁷) ÷ (8.64 × 10⁴) = 3.07 × 10³ m s⁻¹. Every geostationary satellite, no matter what it weighs, has to live at this one radius.
feynman.27: An astronaut “floating” around inside the International Space Station hasn’t escaped Earth’s gravity at all. At the ISS altitude of about 400 km, r = 6.77 × 10⁶ m and g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶)² = 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — still about 89% of what you’d feel on the ground.
feynman.28: The astronaut floats because the station and everything in it are all falling together, every bit of it accelerating at 8.7 m s⁻² toward Earth’s centre. The floor never pushes up on the astronaut’s feet, so the normal force is zero: FN = 0 while Fg is still big. Apparent weightlessness is the missing normal force, not missing gravity.
feynman.29: Trap “There’s no gravity in space” is wrong twice over. The field at ISS altitude is about 89% of its surface value — and without it there’d be no orbit at all; the satellite would just sail off in a straight line at constant speed. Weightlessness in orbit means FN = 0; it never means Fg = 0.
feynman.30: Connects Unit 4 comes back to these very orbits: the time signals from GPS satellites have to be corrected for special relativity because of how fast they’re moving, so the plain circular-orbit model on this page is where a real, measurable relativistic effect starts.

sagan.0: The gravitational field is the first of the fields we will come to know in full: how strong it is, the force it reaches out and exerts, the energy stored in the long climb against it, and the silent, engineless orbits it has sustained for ages without number.
sagan.1: Every mass in the universe drapes the space around it in a gravitational field. The gravitational field strength g at a point is simply the gravitational force the field would exert on each kilogram of mass placed there. For a spherical body of mass M, the strength of that field at a distance r from its centre is:
sagan.2: Field strength is a vector quantity. At every point it points inward, toward the heart of the source mass, so the field of a planet spreads out radially, like spokes converging on a hub. Its magnitude follows the inverse-square law: double your distance from the centre, and the field falls to a quarter of what it was.
sagan.3: Pour in the formula-sheet figures for our own world (M = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg, r = RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m) and we find g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.37 × 10⁶)² = 9.81 N kg⁻¹ at the surface beneath our feet. Climb out to r = 2RE from the centre and the field has weakened to 9.81 ÷ 4 = 2.45 N kg⁻¹; out at 3RE it is fainter still, 9.81 ÷ 9 = 1.09 N kg⁻¹. Within the planet itself the formula no longer holds: there the field dwindles toward zero at the very centre, so the graph of g against r turns a sharp corner at the surface, as shown in Figure 2.1.
sagan.4: The two units of field strength are one and the same: 1 N kg⁻¹ = 1 m s⁻². The force the field exerts on a mass m is Fg = mg, so an object surrendered to free fall accelerates at a = Fg/m = g. The mass cancels away — and herein lies one of the quiet wonders of nature: every object falling freely at the same point quickens at exactly the same rate, be it a feather or a stone, whatever its mass.
sagan.5: Key idea Gravitational field strength at a point is the force per kilogram — and therefore the free-fall acceleration of any object whatsoever — at that point. N kg⁻¹ and m s⁻² are but two names for the same unit.
sagan.6: Connects Every projectile and vertical-circle problem in AOS 1 has been drawing on this very field all along: the steady downward acceleration of 9.8 m s⁻² near the surface is g = GM/r² treated as uniform, because the rise and fall of a thrown ball is vanishingly small set against the great radius RE.
sagan.7: Any two masses in all the cosmos reach toward each other. Newton’s law of universal gravitation gives the magnitude of the force each one exerts upon the other:
sagan.8: These two forces form a Newton’s third law pair: the pull the Earth exerts on the satellite and the pull the satellite exerts on the Earth are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, as shown in Figure 2.2. They never cancel, for they act upon different bodies. The universal law and the picture of the field tell the same story: the force exerted on a mass m where the field strength is g is Fg = mg, since g = GM/r².
sagan.9: Trap A satellite “at an altitude of 630 km” is not at r = 6.30 × 10⁵ m. Altitude h is reckoned from the surface, but r is reckoned all the way from Earth’s centre: r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m. Forgetting to add RE, or adding an altitude in km to a radius in m, is the most common stumble in this topic.

sagan.13: Answer: Fg = 6.09 × 10³ N, directed from the satellite inward toward Earth’s centre. And by Newton’s third law, the satellite returns the favour, exerting a force of 6.09 × 10³ N upon the Earth, directed from Earth’s centre out toward the satellite.

sagan.15: For changes in height that are small beside RE — a thrown ball, a rising lift, a climbing aircraft — this uniform-field approximation serves beautifully. But for a satellite hoisted through thousands of kilometres it breaks down, because g itself fades with altitude. The general method sets aside mgΔh and reaches instead for an area.
sagan.16: The change in gravitational potential energy equals the area under a force–distance graph between the two distances — the very same work-as-area rule we met with springs and trolleys in earlier units. sagan.18: A satellite held in a stable circular orbit feels exactly one force, and one alone: the gravitational pull the central body exerts upon it. Nothing balances it, nothing opposes it. The satellite is forever falling, accelerating inward toward the centre, while its tangential motion carries it around the world rather than down into it. It moves at constant speed yet never at constant velocity, for its direction is changing at every instant.

sagan.22: A geostationary satellite hangs forever above the same point on the equator, so that the dishes below need never turn to follow it. Kenji models its motion as a uniform circular orbit in the plane of the equator, sweeping in the direction of Earth’s own rotation with a period of one day, T = 24 h = 8.64 × 10⁴ s.

sagan.25: Evaluation: r = ∛(7.53 × 10²²) = 4.22 × 10⁷ m, so the altitude is h = r − RE = 4.22 × 10⁷ − 6.37 × 10⁶ = 3.59 × 10⁷ m (some 36 000 km above the surface).

sagan.28: The astronaut floats because the station and everything within it are all falling together, the whole of it accelerating at 8.7 m s⁻² toward Earth’s centre as one. The floor never presses up against the astronaut’s feet, so the normal force is zero: FN = 0 while Fg remains large. Apparent weightlessness is the absence of a normal force, not the absence of gravitational force.
sagan.29: Trap “There is no gravity in space” is wrong twice over. The field at ISS altitude is about 89% of its surface value, and were it not there at all there would be no orbit whatever — the satellite would simply sail off in a straight line at constant speed, out into the dark. sagan.30: Connects Unit 4 will return to these very orbits: the time signals from GPS satellites must be corrected for the effects of special relativity born of their orbital speed, so the humble circular-orbit model on this page is the starting point for an effect we can actually measure.

curie.13: Answer: Fg = 6.09 × 10³ N, directed from the satellite toward Earth’s centre. By Newton’s third law, the satellite exerts a force of 6.09 × 10³ N on Earth, directed from Earth’s centre toward the satellite.
curie.14: Raising a mass through a gravitational field stores gravitational potential energy. Near the surface, where g is effectively constant at 9.81 N kg⁻¹, I write the change as:
curie.15: For height changes that are small compared with RE — a thrown ball, a lift, an aircraft — this uniform-field approximation is excellent. For a satellite raised through thousands of kilometres it fails, because g itself decreases with altitude. The general method replaces mgΔh with an area.
curie.16: The change in gravitational potential energy equals the area under a force–distance graph between the two distances — the same work-as-area rule I used for springs and trolleys in earlier units. If the graph instead shows field strength g against distance, its area carries units of (N kg⁻¹) × m = J kg⁻¹: it gives the energy change per kilogram, and I multiply by the mass m to obtain ΔEg. A field–distance graph is independent of the orbiting object, so one graph serves satellites of every mass.
curie.17: To estimate an area, I count grid squares. First I find the energy value of one square — the force spanned by its height multiplied by the distance spanned by its width — then I count every square that is at least half under the curve. In Figure 2.3 each square represents 500 N × 5.0 × 10⁵ m = 2.5 × 10⁸ J, and about 12.5 squares lie under the curve between r = 7.0 × 10⁶ m and r = 9.0 × 10⁶ m, so ΔEg ≈ 12.5 × 2.5 × 10⁸ ≈ 3.1 × 10⁹ J. I check the axis units before multiplying: distances are often given in km, and axes often carry scale factors such as ×10⁶.
curie.18: A satellite in a stable circular orbit has exactly one force acting on it: the gravitational force exerted on it by the central body. No other force balances it. The satellite accelerates continuously toward the centre while its tangential velocity carries it around rather than down. It travels at constant speed but not constant velocity, because its direction of motion changes at every instant.
curie.19: The gravitational field supplies exactly the centripetal acceleration of uniform circular motion. For a circular orbit of radius r and period T:
curie.20: Setting the field strength equal to the centripetal acceleration ties the orbit to the central mass: GM/r² = v²/r = 4π²r/T². The satellite’s own mass appears nowhere in this equation. For a given central body, choosing the orbital radius fixes the speed and the period — a bolt and a space station at the same radius orbit identically.
curie.21: Key idea Orbital speed, period and acceleration depend only on the central mass M and the orbital radius r — never on the mass of the satellite.
curie.22: A geostationary satellite remains above the same point on the equator, so receiving dishes never need to track it. Kenji models the satellite’s motion as a uniform circular orbit in the plane of the equator, travelling in the direction of Earth’s rotation with a period of one day, T = 24 h = 8.64 × 10⁴ s.
curie.23: Formula: the field supplies the centripetal acceleration, G Mr² = 4π²rT², which rearranges to r³ = GMT²4π².
curie.25: Evaluation: r = ∛(7.53 × 10²²) = 4.22 × 10⁷ m, so the altitude is h = r − RE = 4.22 × 10⁷ − 6.37 × 10⁶ = 3.59 × 10⁷ m (about 36 000 km above the surface).
curie.26: Answer: the orbital speed is v = 2πr/T = (2π × 4.22 × 10⁷) ÷ (8.64 × 10⁴) = 3.07 × 10³ m s⁻¹. Every geostationary satellite, whatever its mass, must occupy this one radius.
curie.27: An astronaut “floating” aboard the International Space Station has not escaped Earth’s gravitational field. At the ISS altitude of about 400 km, r = 6.77 × 10⁶ m and g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶)² = 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — about 89% of the surface value.
curie.28: The astronaut floats because the station and everything inside it are in free fall together, all accelerating at 8.7 m s⁻² toward Earth’s centre. The floor never presses on the astronaut’s feet, so the normal force is zero: FN = 0 while Fg remains large. Apparent weightlessness is the absence of a normal force, not the absence of gravitational force.

curie.30: Connects Unit 4 returns to these orbits: time signals from GPS satellites must be corrected for the effects of special relativity due to their orbital speed, so the circular-orbit model on this page is the starting point for a measurable relativistic effect.

cox.0: The gravitational field is the first field we get to treat properly, with numbers: how strong it is, the force it exerts, the energy you store simply by climbing through it, and the orbits it holds steady, endlessly, without any engine at all.
cox.1: Every mass, quietly, sits at the centre of its own gravitational field. The gravitational field strength g at a point is simply the gravitational force the field would exert on each kilogram of mass you placed there. For a spherical body of mass M, the field strength at a distance r from its centre is:
cox.2: Field strength is a vector. At every point it reaches inward, toward the centre of the source mass, so the field of a planet is radial, pointing home from every direction. And the magnitude follows that lovely inverse-square law: double your distance from the centre, and the field drops not to a half but to a quarter.
cox.3: If we drop in the formula-sheet data for Earth (M = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg, r = RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m) we get g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.37 × 10⁶)² = 9.81 N kg⁻¹ at the surface. Move out to r = 2RE from the centre and the field has fallen to 9.81 ÷ 4 = 2.45 N kg⁻¹, and at 3RE it is only 9.81 ÷ 9 = 1.09 N kg⁻¹. Inside the planet the formula simply does not apply: the field eases away toward zero at the very centre, so the graph of g against r turns a sharp corner at the surface, as shown in Figure 2.1.
cox.4: The two units of field strength are really the same thing: 1 N kg⁻¹ = 1 m s⁻². The force the field exerts on a mass m is Fg = mg, so an object falling freely has acceleration a = Fg/m = g. And here is the wonderful part: the mass cancels. Every object in free fall at the same point accelerates at exactly the same rate, no matter how heavy it is.
cox.5: Key idea Gravitational field strength at a point is the force per kilogram — and therefore the free-fall acceleration of any object you care to drop — at that point. N kg⁻¹ and m s⁻² are one and the same unit.
cox.6: Connects Every projectile and vertical-circle problem back in AOS 1 leans on this very field: that steady downward 9.8 m s⁻² near the surface is g = GM/r², which we are happy to treat as uniform because the height changes are so tiny compared with RE.
cox.7: Any two masses, anywhere, pull on each other. Newton’s law of universal gravitation gives the size of the force each one feels from the other:
cox.8: The two forces are a Newton’s third law pair: the force Earth exerts on the satellite and the force the satellite exerts on Earth are equal in size and opposite in direction, as shown in Figure 2.2. They never cancel, because they act on different objects. And the universal law and the field picture quietly agree: the force on a mass m at a point where the field strength is g is Fg = mg, since g = GM/r².

It moves at constant speed but not constant velocity, because its direction is changing at every single instant.
cox.19: The gravitational field provides precisely the centripetal acceleration that uniform circular motion demands — no more, no less. For a circular orbit of radius r and period T:

greene.0: Of all the fields we will meet, gravity is the one we feel in our bones, and it is the first we will pin down with numbers. Picture everything we are about to ask of it: how strong it is at a given place, the force it presses on whatever sits there, the energy quietly banked by climbing through it, and—most remarkable of all—the orbits it holds in place forever, without any engine, without any effort at all.
greene.1: Every mass drapes the space around it with a gravitational field. The gravitational field strength g at a point is simply the gravitational force the field would exert on each kilogram of mass placed there. For a spherical body of mass M, the strength of that field at a distance r from its centre is:
greene.2: Field strength is a vector, and that matters. At every point it aims straight back toward the centre of the source mass, so a planet's field fans outward like the spokes of a wheel—radial in every direction. Its magnitude follows the inverse-square law, and here is the consequence worth holding onto: double your distance from the centre and the field drops not to a half but to a quarter.
greene.3: Let us put Earth's own numbers in. Taking the formula-sheet data (M = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg, r = RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m) gives g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.37 × 10⁶)² = 9.81 N kg⁻¹ at the surface. Step outward to r = 2RE from the centre and the field falls to 9.81 ÷ 4 = 2.45 N kg⁻¹; out at 3RE it is gentler still, 9.81 ÷ 9 = 1.09 N kg⁻¹. Burrow inward, beneath the surface, and the formula quietly stops applying: the field now sinks toward zero at the very centre. So the graph of g against r turns a sharp corner exactly at the surface, as shown in Figure 2.1.
greene.4: The two units of field strength are one and the same: 1 N kg⁻¹ = 1 m s⁻². Watch why. The force the field exerts on a mass m is Fg = mg, so an object released into free fall accelerates at a = Fg/m = g. The mass cancels—and in that small cancellation lies one of nature's quiet wonders: at any given point, every falling object speeds up at exactly the same rate, no matter how heavy or how light it is.
greene.5: Key idea Gravitational field strength at a point is the force per kilogram—and therefore the free-fall acceleration of any object—at that point. N kg⁻¹ and m s⁻² are the same unit.
greene.6: Connects Every projectile and vertical-circle problem back in AOS 1 was quietly leaning on this very field. That steady downward 9.8 m s⁻² near the surface is nothing other than g = GM/r², treated as uniform simply because the heights involved are tiny next to RE.
greene.7: Reach for any two masses anywhere in the universe and they pull on each other. Newton's law of universal gravitation gives the magnitude of the force each one exerts on the other:
greene.8: These two forces form a Newton's third law pair: the pull Earth exerts on the satellite and the pull the satellite exerts on Earth are equal in magnitude and exactly opposite in direction, as shown in Figure 2.2. They never cancel one another, for the simple reason that they act on different objects. nye.0: Now check this out! The gravitational field is the very first field we get to nail down with real numbers — how strong it is, the force it exerts, the energy you store just by moving through it, and the orbits it keeps spinning along without a single engine running. None at all!
nye.1: Here’s the deal: every mass out there is wrapped in a gravitational field. The gravitational field strength g at a point is just the gravitational force the field exerts on each and every kilogram of mass you drop in there. And for a nice round body of mass M, the field strength out at distance r from its centre comes out to:
nye.2: Now field strength is a vector — it’s got direction! At every single point it aims straight in toward the centre of the source mass, so a planet’s field points inward all around, like spokes on a wheel. And the size of it follows the inverse-square law: double your distance from the centre and watch the field drop to a quarter of what it was!
nye.3: Let’s plug in the formula-sheet numbers for Earth (M = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg, r = RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m) and see what pops out: g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.37 × 10⁶)² = 9.81 N kg⁻¹ right at the surface. Move out to r = 2RE from the centre and the field drops to 9.81 ÷ 4 = 2.45 N kg⁻¹, and at 3RE it’s down to 9.81 ÷ 9 = 1.09 N kg⁻¹. But get this — once you head inside the planet the formula quits on you: the field falls away toward zero at the very centre, so the graph of g against r has a sharp corner right at the surface, like you can see in Figure 2.1.
nye.4: And here’s a neat one — those two units of field strength are exactly the same thing: 1 N kg⁻¹ = 1 m s⁻². The force the field exerts on a mass m is Fg = mg, so a freely falling object accelerates at a = Fg/m = g. Watch what happens — the mass cancels right out! Every object in free fall at the same point speeds up at the exact same rate, no matter how heavy or light it is!
nye.5: Key idea Gravitational field strength at a point is the force per kilogram — which is the very same thing as the free-fall acceleration of any object — sitting at that point. And N kg⁻¹ and m s⁻² are one and the same unit!
nye.6: Connects Every projectile problem and every vertical-circle problem back in AOS 1 leans on this exact same field: that steady downward acceleration of 9.8 m s⁻² near the surface is just g = GM/r² treated as uniform, because the height changes are teeny compared with RE.
nye.7: Any two masses pull on each other — every pair! Newton’s law of universal gravitation hands you the size of the force each one exerts on the other:
nye.8: Now these two forces team up as a Newton’s third law pair: the force Earth exerts on the satellite and the force the satellite exerts right back on Earth are equal in size and pointed opposite ways, like you see in Figure 2.2. And here’s the thing — they never cancel out, because they’re acting on different objects! The universal law and the field picture totally agree with each other: the force exerted on a mass m sitting where the field strength is g comes out to Fg = mg, since g = GM/r².
nye.9: Trap Don’t get fooled — a satellite “at an altitude of 630 km” is not sitting at r = 6.30 × 10⁵ m! Altitude h gets measured from the surface, but r gets measured all the way from Earth’s centre: r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m. Forgetting to add RE, or tossing an altitude in km onto a radius in m, is the number-one slip-up in this whole topic!
nye.10: Picture this: Lucia is tracking a 750 kg communications satellite cruising in a circular orbit at an altitude of 6.30 × 10⁵ m. She treats the satellite as a point mass and grabs the formula-sheet data for Earth.
nye.11: Formula: Fg = G m1m2r² with r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m.
nye.13: Answer: Fg = 6.09 × 10³ N, pointed from the satellite straight toward Earth’s centre. And by Newton’s third law, the satellite exerts a force of 6.09 × 10³ N right back on Earth, aimed from Earth’s centre out toward the satellite!
nye.14: Lift a mass up through a gravitational field and you bank some gravitational potential energy. Near the surface, where g stays pretty much locked at 9.81 N kg⁻¹, the change works out to:
nye.15: For height changes that stay small next to RE — a tossed ball, a lift ride, an aircraft — this uniform-field shortcut is fantastic. But for a satellite hauled up through thousands of kilometres it falls apart, because g itself shrinks as you climb. So the general method swaps out mgΔh for an area!
nye.16: The change in gravitational potential energy equals the area under a force–distance graph between the two distances — the very same work-as-area rule you used for springs and trolleys back in earlier units! And if the graph shows field strength g against distance instead, its area carries units of (N kg⁻¹) × m = J kg⁻¹: it hands you the energy change per kilogram, and you multiply by the mass m to get ΔEg. Here’s the beauty of it — a field–distance graph doesn’t care about the orbiting object at all, so one single graph works for satellites of every mass!
nye.17: Want to estimate an area? Count grid squares! First figure out the energy value of one square — the force spanned by its height times the distance spanned by its width — then count up every square that’s at least half tucked under the curve. In Figure 2.3 each square stands for 500 N × 5.0 × 10⁵ m = 2.5 × 10⁸ J, and about 12.5 squares sit under the curve between r = 7.0 × 10⁶ m and r = 9.0 × 10⁶ m, so ΔEg ≈ 12.5 × 2.5 × 10⁸ ≈ 3.1 × 10⁹ J. And always check those axis units before you multiply — distances love to show up in km, and axes often sneak in scale factors like ×10⁶!
nye.18: Now get this — a satellite in a steady circular orbit has exactly one force on it: the gravitational pull the central body exerts on it. That’s it! Nothing else balances it out. The satellite keeps accelerating in toward the centre the whole time, while its sideways velocity carries it around instead of straight down. It travels at constant speed but not constant velocity, because its direction of motion keeps changing at every single instant!

Look — the satellite’s own mass shows up nowhere in this equation! For a given central body, once you pick the orbital radius you’ve locked in the speed and the period — a loose bolt and a giant space station at the same radius orbit in exactly the same way!

nye.28: So why does she float? Because the station and everything packed inside it are all in free fall together, every bit of it accelerating at 8.7 m s⁻² toward Earth’s centre. Apparent weightlessness is the absence of a normal force, not the absence of gravitational force.

einstein.0: Here is the first field I shall ask you to measure rather than merely feel: the gravitational field. We will ask how strong it is, what force it presses upon a mass set within it, what energy is laid up by carrying a body through it, and how it holds the worlds in their orbits without any engine turning at all.
einstein.1: Every mass, I have come to see, carries about it a gravitational field. The gravitational field strength g at a point is simply the gravitational pull felt by each kilogram of matter I place there. For a round body of mass M, the field at a distance r from its centre is:
einstein.2: Field strength is a vector quantity. At every point it aims straight inward, toward the heart of the source mass, so the field of a planet spreads out like spokes from a hub — it is radial. Its size follows the inverse-square law: let me double my distance from the centre, and the field falls to a quarter of what it was.
einstein.3: Let me put in the figures from the formula sheet for the Earth (M = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg, r = RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m), and I find g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.37 × 10⁶)² = 9.81 N kg⁻¹ at the surface. Stepping out to r = 2RE from the centre, the field has dwindled to 9.81 ÷ 4 = 2.45 N kg⁻¹, and at 3RE to 9.81 ÷ 9 = 1.09 N kg⁻¹. Inside the planet, mind you, the formula no longer holds: there the field sinks toward zero at the very centre, so the graph of g against r bends with a sharp corner at the surface, as I have drawn in Figure 2.1.
einstein.4: The two units of field strength are one and the same: 1 N kg⁻¹ = 1 m s⁻². The force the field lays upon a mass m is Fg = mg, so a body let fall freely takes on the acceleration a = Fg/m = g. The mass cancels clean away — and here is the marvel I never tire of: every object falling freely at the same point quickens at the very same rate, be it heavy or light.
einstein.5: Key idea Gravitational field strength at a point is the force borne by each kilogram — and so, equally, the free-fall acceleration of any object whatsoever — at that point. N kg⁻¹ and m s⁻² are but two names for the same unit.
einstein.6: Connects Every projectile and every vertical-circle puzzle in AOS 1 leans on this same field: that steady downward acceleration of 9.8 m s⁻² near the surface is nothing but g = GM/r² taken as uniform, because the little changes in height are as nothing beside RE.
einstein.7: Any two masses reach out and draw one another close. Newton’s law of universal gravitation tells me the size of the force each lays upon the other:
einstein.8: These two forces make a Newton’s third law pair: the pull the Earth lays on the satellite and the pull the satellite lays on the Earth are equal in size and opposite in direction, as I have shown in Figure 2.2. They can never cancel, for they take hold of different bodies. The universal law and the picture of the field tell one story: the force upon a mass m at a place where the field strength is g is Fg = mg, since g = GM/r².
einstein.9: Trap A satellite “at an altitude of 630 km” is not sitting at r = 6.30 × 10⁵ m. Altitude h I measure up from the surface, but r I must measure from the Earth’s very centre: r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m. To forget to add RE, or to add an altitude in km onto a radius in m, is the commonest stumble in this whole topic.
einstein.10: Lucia keeps watch on a 750 kg communications satellite circling in a round orbit at an altitude of 6.30 × 10⁵ m. She treats the satellite as a single point of mass and takes the Earth’s figures from the formula sheet.
einstein.11: Formula: Fg = G m1m2r² with r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m.
einstein.13: Answer: Fg = 6.09 × 10³ N, aimed from the satellite toward the Earth’s centre. And by Newton’s third law, the satellite in its turn lays a force of 6.09 × 10³ N upon the Earth, aimed from the Earth’s centre out toward the satellite.
einstein.14: To lift a mass up through a gravitational field is to lay up gravitational potential energy within it. Near the surface, where g holds steady at 9.81 N kg⁻¹, the change is:
einstein.15: For changes of height that are small beside RE — a tossed ball, a rising lift, an aircraft — this picture of a uniform field serves beautifully. But for a satellite hoisted through thousands of kilometres it breaks down, because g itself thins out with altitude. The general way around this is to set aside mgΔh and reach instead for an area.
einstein.16: The change in gravitational potential energy is the area under a force–distance graph taken between the two distances — the very same work-as-area rule I used for springs and trolleys in the earlier units. And if the graph shows me field strength g against distance instead, its area carries the units (N kg⁻¹) × m = J kg⁻¹: it hands me the energy change for each kilogram, and must be multiplied by the mass m to give ΔEg. A field–distance graph cares nothing for the orbiting body, so one graph alone will serve satellites of every mass.
einstein.17: To guess at an area, I count the little squares of the grid. First let me find what one square is worth — the force its height spans, multiplied by the distance its width spans — and then I count every square that lies at least half beneath the curve. In Figure 2.3 each square stands for 500 N × 5.0 × 10⁵ m = 2.5 × 10⁸ J, and some 12.5 squares lie under the curve between r = 7.0 × 10⁶ m and r = 9.0 × 10⁶ m, so ΔEg ≈ 12.5 × 2.5 × 10⁸ ≈ 3.1 × 10⁹ J. But look hard at the units on the axes before you multiply: distances often come dressed in km, and axes often carry scale factors such as ×10⁶.
einstein.18: A satellite in a steady round orbit feels just one force, and no more: the gravitational pull laid upon it by the central body. Nothing else stands against it. The satellite is forever quickening inward, toward the centre, while its sideways velocity carries it around rather than letting it fall straight down. It moves at constant speed yet not at constant velocity, for the way it points is changing in every instant.
einstein.19: The gravitational field gives over exactly the centripetal acceleration that uniform circular motion demands. For a round orbit of radius r and period T:
einstein.20: Set the field strength equal to the centripetal acceleration, and at once the orbit is bound fast to the central mass: GM/r² = v²/r = 4π²r/T². Nowhere in this equation does the satellite’s own mass so much as appear. For a given central body, once I choose the orbital radius the speed and the period are settled — a loose bolt and a space station at the same radius circle in exactly the same way.
einstein.21: Key idea Orbital speed, period and acceleration hang only upon the central mass M and the orbital radius r — never upon the mass of the satellite.
einstein.22: A geostationary satellite hangs forever above one same spot on the equator, so the receiving dishes below need never turn to follow it. Kenji pictures the satellite’s motion as a uniform round orbit lying in the plane of the equator, travelling the way the Earth itself turns, with a period of one whole day, T = 24 h = 8.64 × 10⁴ s.
einstein.23: Formula: the field gives over the centripetal acceleration, G Mr² = 4π²rT², which rearranges to r³ = GMT²4π².
einstein.25: Evaluation: r = ∛(7.53 × 10²²) = 4.22 × 10⁷ m, so the altitude works out to h = r − RE = 4.22 × 10⁷ − 6.37 × 10⁶ = 3.59 × 10⁷ m (some 36 000 km above the surface).
einstein.26: Answer: the orbital speed is v = 2πr/T = (2π × 4.22 × 10⁷) ÷ (8.64 × 10⁴) = 3.07 × 10³ m s⁻¹. Every geostationary satellite, whatever its mass may be, must take up this one same radius.
einstein.27: An astronaut “floating” within the International Space Station has by no means slipped free of the Earth’s gravitational field. At the altitude of the ISS, some 400 km up, r = 6.77 × 10⁶ m and g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶)² = 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — about 89% of what it is at the surface.
einstein.28: The astronaut floats because the station and all within it are falling freely together, every one of them quickening at 8.7 m s⁻² toward the Earth’s centre. The floor never presses up against the astronaut’s feet, so the normal force is nothing at all: FN = 0 while Fg stays large. Apparent weightlessness is the absence of a normal force, not the absence of gravitational force.
einstein.29: Trap “There is no gravity in space” is wrong twice over. The field at the ISS altitude is about 89% of its value at the surface, and were it not there at all there would be no orbit whatsoever — the satellite would sail off in a straight line at constant speed. Weightlessness in orbit means FN = 0; never does it mean Fg = 0.
einstein.30: Connects Unit 4 comes back to these orbits: the time signals sent down from GPS satellites must be set right for the effects of special relativity owing to their orbital speed, so the circular-orbit model on this page is where a measurable relativistic effect first takes its start.

Inside the planet the formula loses its footing: the field falls toward zero at the very centre, so the graph of g against r turns a sharp corner at the surface, as shown in Figure 2.1.
levin.4: The two units of field strength are one and the same: 1 N kg⁻¹ = 1 m s⁻². The force the field lays on a mass m is Fg = mg, so anything in free fall accelerates at a = Fg/m = g. The mass cancels — it simply leaves — and so every object falling freely at the same point falls at the same rate, no matter how heavy or how light.
levin.5: Key idea Gravitational field strength at a point is the force per kilogram — and therefore the free-fall acceleration of anything — at that point. N kg⁻¹ and m s⁻² are the same unit wearing two names.
levin.6: Connects Every projectile, every vertical-circle problem in AOS 1 leans on this one field. That steady downward acceleration of 9.8 m s⁻² near the surface is nothing but g = GM/r², treated as uniform because the rise and fall are tiny set against RE.
levin.7: Any two masses reach for each other. Newton’s law of universal gravitation names the magnitude of the force each lays on the other:
levin.8: The two forces are a Newton’s third law pair: the force Earth lays on the satellite and the force the satellite lays back on Earth are equal in size, opposite in direction, as shown in Figure 2.2. They never cancel, because they fall on different bodies. The universal law and the field picture say the same thing in two tongues: the force on a mass m where the field strength is g is Fg = mg, since g = GM/r².
levin.9: Trap A satellite “at an altitude of 630 km” is not sitting at r = 6.30 × 10⁵ m. Altitude h is measured up from the surface; r is measured out from Earth’s centre: r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m. Forgetting to add RE, or folding an altitude in km into a radius in m, is the oldest mistake in this topic.
levin.10: Lucia is tracking a 750 kg communications satellite circling at an altitude of 6.30 × 10⁵ m. She treats the satellite as a single point of mass and reaches for the formula-sheet data for Earth.
levin.11: Formula: Fg = G m1m2r² with r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m.
levin.13: Answer: Fg = 6.09 × 10³ N, aimed from the satellite down toward Earth’s centre. And by Newton’s third law, the satellite answers back: it lays a force of 6.09 × 10³ N on Earth, aimed from Earth’s centre out toward the satellite.
levin.14: Lift a mass through a gravitational field and you store gravitational potential energy in the climb. Near the surface, where g holds steady at 9.81 N kg⁻¹, the change is:
levin.15: For climbs that are small set against RE — a thrown ball, a lift, an aircraft — this uniform-field shortcut is faithful. For a satellite hauled up through thousands of kilometres it breaks, because g itself thins out with altitude. The general method trades mgΔh for an area.
levin.16: The change in gravitational potential energy is the area under a force–distance graph between the two distances — the same work-as-area rule that served springs and trolleys in earlier units. If the graph plots field strength g against distance instead, its area carries units of (N kg⁻¹) × m = J kg⁻¹: it tells the energy change per kilogram, and must be multiplied by the mass m to give ΔEg. A field–distance graph cares nothing for the orbiting object, so one graph speaks for satellites of every mass.
levin.17: To read an area, count grid squares. First learn the energy each square is worth — the force spanned by its height times the distance spanned by its width — then count every square at least half-drowned under the curve. In Figure 2.3 each square stands for 500 N × 5.0 × 10⁵ m = 2.5 × 10⁸ J, and about 12.5 squares lie beneath the curve between r = 7.0 × 10⁶ m and r = 9.0 × 10⁶ m, so ΔEg ≈ 12.5 × 2.5 × 10⁸ ≈ 3.1 × 10⁹ J. Read the axis units before you multiply: distances arrive in km more often than not, and axes wear quiet scale factors like ×10⁶.
levin.18: A satellite in a stable circular orbit feels exactly one force, and one alone: the gravitational pull of the central body. Nothing balances it. The satellite falls toward the centre without rest, while its sideways velocity carries it around rather than down. Its speed never changes; its velocity changes always, because the direction it moves turns at every instant.
levin.19: The gravitational field hands over precisely the centripetal acceleration that uniform circular motion demands. For a circular orbit of radius r and period T:
levin.20: Set the field strength equal to the centripetal acceleration and the orbit is bound to the central mass: GM/r² = v²/r = 4π²r/T². Look for the satellite’s own mass in that equation; it is nowhere to be found. For a given central body, choose the orbital radius and you have fixed the speed and the period both — a loose bolt and a space station at the same radius orbit as one.
levin.21: Key idea Orbital speed, period and acceleration answer only to the central mass M and the orbital radius r — never to the mass of the satellite.
levin.22: A geostationary satellite hangs above one fixed point on the equator, so a receiving dish can stare and never turn. Kenji models its motion as a uniform circular orbit in the plane of the equator, running with Earth’s own spin, completing one circuit in a day, T = 24 h = 8.64 × 10⁴ s.
levin.23: Formula: the field supplies the centripetal acceleration, G Mr² = 4π²rT², which rearranges to r³ = GMT²4π².
levin.25: Evaluation: r = ∛(7.53 × 10²²) = 4.22 × 10⁷ m, so the altitude comes out to h = r − RE = 4.22 × 10⁷ − 6.37 × 10⁶ = 3.59 × 10⁷ m (some 36 000 km above the surface).
levin.26: Answer: the orbital speed is v = 2πr/T = (2π × 4.22 × 10⁷) ÷ (8.64 × 10⁴) = 3.07 × 10³ m s⁻¹. Every geostationary satellite, heavy or light, must keep to this one radius — there is room for only one.
levin.27: An astronaut “floating” aboard the International Space Station has not slipped free of Earth’s gravitational field. At the ISS altitude of about 400 km, r = 6.77 × 10⁶ m and g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶)² = 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — near enough 89% of the value down at the surface.
levin.28: The astronaut floats because the station and everything within it are falling together, all of them accelerating at 8.7 m s⁻² toward Earth’s centre. The floor never pushes back against the astronaut’s feet, so the normal force is nothing: FN = 0 while Fg stays large. Apparent weightlessness is the absence of a normal force, not the absence of gravitational force.
levin.29: Trap “There is no gravity in space” is wrong twice over. The field at ISS altitude is about 89% of its surface value — and were it truly gone, there would be no orbit at all; the satellite would fly off straight and steady into the dark. Weightlessness in orbit means FN = 0; it never means Fg = 0.
levin.30: Connects Unit 4 comes back to these orbits. The time signals from GPS satellites must be nudged to account for special relativity, born of their orbital speed — so the plain circular-orbit model on this page is where a measurable relativistic effect begins.

czerski.0: Gravity is the first field we get to pin down with numbers — and it’s a generous one to start with. We can ask how strong it is, how hard it pushes on whatever sits in it, how much energy you bank by hauling something up through it, and how it can keep a moon or a satellite swinging round and round forever without so much as a flicker of an engine.
czerski.1: Every lump of mass drapes a gravitational field around itself, you included. The gravitational field strength g at a point is simply the gravitational tug felt by each kilogram of mass you place there. For a round body of mass M, the field strength a distance r out from its centre comes to:
czerski.2: Field strength is a vector — it has a direction as well as a size. Everywhere it points straight in toward the centre of the source mass, so a planet’s field spreads out like the spokes of a wheel, radial in every direction. And its size follows the inverse-square law: step twice as far from the centre and the field drops to a quarter of what it was.

Burrow inside the planet, though, and the formula gives up: there the field falls away toward zero at the very centre, so the graph of g against r has a sharp kink right at the surface, like the one in Figure 2.1.
czerski.4: Those two units of field strength are really the same thing: 1 N kg⁻¹ = 1 m s⁻². The force the field lays on a mass m is Fg = mg, so anything in free fall accelerates at a = Fg/m = g. The universal law and the field picture tell the same story: the force on a mass m sitting where the field strength is g works out to Fg = mg, because g = GM/r².

attenborough.6: Connects Every projectile and vertical-circle problem in AOS 1 leans upon this same field: that steady downward pull of 9.8 m s⁻² near the surface is g = GM/r² treated as uniform, because the rise and fall are so slight against the great bulk of RE.
attenborough.7: Any two masses, anywhere in the universe, reach out and draw one another close. Newton’s law of universal gravitation gives the size of the force each lays upon the other:
attenborough.8: These two forces are partners in a Newton’s third law pair: the pull of Earth upon the satellite, and the pull of the satellite upon Earth, are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, as Figure 2.2 shows. They never cancel, for they act upon different bodies. The universal law and the field picture tell the same story: the force upon a mass m, at a place where the field strength is g, is Fg = mg, since g = GM/r².
attenborough.9: Trap A satellite “at an altitude of 630 km” does not sit at r = 6.30 × 10⁵ m. Altitude h is reckoned from the surface, but r is reckoned from Earth’s very centre: r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m. To forget the RE, or to add an altitude in km to a radius in m, is the commonest stumble in all this topic.
attenborough.10: Lucia keeps watch on a 750 kg communications satellite, gliding in a circular orbit at an altitude of 6.30 × 10⁵ m. She treats the satellite as a single point of mass and draws upon the formula-sheet data for Earth.
attenborough.11: Formula: Fg = G m1m2r² with r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m.
attenborough.13: Answer: Fg = 6.09 × 10³ N, directed from the satellite toward Earth’s centre. And by Newton’s third law, the satellite returns the favour, pulling on Earth with a force of 6.09 × 10³ N, directed from Earth’s centre toward the satellite.
attenborough.14: To lift a mass through a gravitational field is to store gravitational potential energy within it. Near the surface, where g holds all but constant at 9.81 N kg⁻¹, the change is:
attenborough.15: For rises and falls that are slight against RE — a thrown ball, a lift, an aircraft — this uniform-field approximation serves beautifully. But for a satellite hauled up through thousands of kilometres it falters, because g itself grows weaker with every kilometre of altitude. The general method sets aside mgΔh and turns instead to an area.
attenborough.16: The change in gravitational potential energy is the area under a force–distance graph between the two distances — the very same work-as-area rule we used for springs and trolleys in earlier units. Should the graph instead trace field strength g against distance, its area carries the units (N kg⁻¹) × m = J kg⁻¹: it tells us the energy change per kilogram, and must be multiplied by the mass m to yield ΔEg. A field–distance graph cares nothing for the orbiting object, so a single graph will serve satellites of every mass alike.
attenborough.17: To estimate an area, we count the grid squares. First find the energy held in a single square — the force spanned by its height multiplied by the distance spanned by its width — then count every square that lies at least half beneath the curve. In Figure 2.3 each square stands for 500 N × 5.0 × 10⁵ m = 2.5 × 10⁸ J, and about 12.5 squares lie under the curve between r = 7.0 × 10⁶ m and r = 9.0 × 10⁶ m, so ΔEg ≈ 12.5 × 2.5 × 10⁸ ≈ 3.1 × 10⁹ J. Look closely at the axis units before you multiply: distances are so often given in km, and the axes so often carry scale factors such as ×10⁶.
attenborough.18: A satellite, settled in a stable circular orbit, feels but a single force: the gravitational pull of the central body upon it. Nothing else balances that pull. Forever the satellite quickens toward the centre, and yet its tangential velocity carries it around rather than down. It travels at constant speed but never at constant velocity, for the direction of its journey turns at every passing instant.
attenborough.19: The gravitational field provides, exactly, the centripetal acceleration that uniform circular motion demands. For a circular orbit of radius r and period T:
attenborough.20: Set the field strength equal to the centripetal acceleration, and the orbit is bound fast to the central mass: GM/r² = v²/r = 4π²r/T². The satellite’s own mass appears nowhere in this equation. For a given central body, to choose the orbital radius is to fix the speed and the period at a stroke — a loose bolt and a space station, sharing the same radius, orbit as one.
attenborough.21: Key idea Orbital speed, period and acceleration depend only upon the central mass M and the orbital radius r — never upon the mass of the satellite.
attenborough.22: A geostationary satellite hangs forever above the same point on the equator, so the receiving dishes below need never turn to follow it. Kenji pictures the satellite’s motion as a uniform circular orbit in the plane of the equator, sweeping in the direction of Earth’s own rotation, with a period of one day, T = 24 h = 8.64 × 10⁴ s.
attenborough.23: Formula: the field provides the centripetal acceleration, G Mr² = 4π²rT², which rearranges to r³ = GMT²4π².
attenborough.25: Evaluation: r = ∛(7.53 × 10²²) = 4.22 × 10⁷ m, so the altitude is h = r − RE = 4.22 × 10⁷ − 6.37 × 10⁶ = 3.59 × 10⁷ m (some 36 000 km above the surface).
attenborough.26: Answer: the orbital speed is v = 2πr/T = (2π × 4.22 × 10⁷) ÷ (8.64 × 10⁴) = 3.07 × 10³ m s⁻¹. Every geostationary satellite, whatever its mass, must keep to this one radius and no other.
attenborough.27: An astronaut “floating” aboard the International Space Station has not escaped Earth’s gravitational field at all. At the ISS altitude of some 400 km, r = 6.77 × 10⁶ m and g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶)² = 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — still about 89% of the value at the surface.
attenborough.28: The astronaut floats because the station and all within it are falling together, every one of them quickening at 8.7 m s⁻² toward Earth’s centre. The floor never presses upon the astronaut’s feet, and so the normal force is nothing at all: FN = 0, while Fg remains large. Apparent weightlessness is the absence of a normal force, not the absence of gravitational force.
attenborough.29: Trap “There is no gravity in space” is wrong twice over. The field at ISS altitude is about 89% of its surface value, and without it there would be no orbit at all — the satellite would simply sail off in a straight line at constant speed. Weightlessness in orbit means FN = 0; it never, ever means Fg = 0.
attenborough.30: Connects Unit 4 will return to these very orbits: the time signals from GPS satellites must be corrected for the effects of special relativity born of their orbital speed, so the humble circular-orbit model on this page is the seed of a real, measurable relativistic effect.

greekepic.0: Sing now of the first of the unseen fields to be weighed and numbered by mortal hands: of its strength, of the force it lays upon all things, of the energy hoarded by any traveller who climbs against it, and of the orbits it upholds for ever, turning without engine, without oar, without any toil at all.
greekepic.1: About every mass there dwells a gravitational field, an invisible robe upon the world. The gravitational field strength g at a point is the gravitational force borne by each kilogram of mass set down at that point. For a spherical body of mass M, the strength of the field at distance r from its centre is declared thus:
greekepic.2: Field strength is a vector, possessed of bearing as well as might. At every point it is turned inward, toward the centre of the source mass, so that the field of a planet stands radial, like spokes drawn to a hub. Its magnitude keeps faith with the inverse-square law: double the distance from the centre, and the field is quartered.
greekepic.3: Take up the data graven on the formula sheet for the Earth (M = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg, r = RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m) and there comes forth g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.37 × 10⁶)² = 9.81 N kg⁻¹ at the surface. At r = 2RE from the centre the field is weakened to 9.81 ÷ 4 = 2.45 N kg⁻¹, and at 3RE to 9.81 ÷ 9 = 1.09 N kg⁻¹. Yet within the body of the planet the formula holds no longer: there the field sinks toward nothing at the very centre, so that the graph of g against r bears a sharp corner at the surface, as is shown in Figure 2.1.
greekepic.4: Two units name the same strength, and they are as one: 1 N kg⁻¹ = 1 m s⁻². The force the field lays upon a mass m is Fg = mg, so that a thing falling freely is given the acceleration a = Fg/m = g. Behold, the mass cancels and departs — every object in free fall at one point quickens at the selfsame rate, be it heavy or light, mighty or mean.
greekepic.5: Key idea The gravitational field strength at a point is the force borne by each kilogram — and therefore the free-fall acceleration of any object whatsoever — at that point. N kg⁻¹ and m s⁻² are one unit under two names.
greekepic.6: Connects Every problem of the projectile and the vertical circle in AOS 1 leans upon this same field: that steadfast downward acceleration of 9.8 m s⁻² near the surface is g = GM/r² counted as uniform, for the changes of height are but trifles set beside RE.
greekepic.7: Any two masses reach out and draw each toward the other. Newton's law of universal gravitation proclaims the magnitude of the force each lays upon its fellow:
greekepic.8: These two forces stand as a pair after Newton's third law: the force the Earth lays upon the satellite and the force the satellite lays upon the Earth are equal in might and opposed in bearing, as is shown in Figure 2.2. Never do they cancel, for they fall upon different bodies. The universal law and the picture of the field speak with one voice: the force borne by a mass m at a point where the field strength is g is Fg = mg, since g = GM/r².
greekepic.9: Trap A satellite “at an altitude of 630 km” is not to be found at r = 6.30 × 10⁵ m. To forget the adding of RE, or to join an altitude in km to a radius in m, is the most common stumbling in this whole topic.

greekepic.14: To raise a mass up through a gravitational field is to hoard within it gravitational potential energy. Near the surface, where g stands all but unchanging at 9.81 N kg⁻¹, the change is reckoned thus:

But for a satellite lifted through thousands of kilometres it fails, for g itself wanes with altitude. The general method then casts aside mgΔh and sets an area in its place.

And if instead the graph should show field strength g against distance, its area bears the units (N kg⁻¹) × m = J kg⁻¹: it yields the change of energy for each kilogram, and must be multiplied by the mass m to give forth ΔEg. greekepic.17: To reckon an area, number the squares of the grid. First find the worth of a single square — the force spanned by its height multiplied by the distance spanned by its breadth — then number every square that lies at least half beneath the curve. In Figure 2.3 each square stands for 500 N × 5.0 × 10⁵ m = 2.5 × 10⁸ J, and some 12.5 squares lie beneath the curve between r = 7.0 × 10⁶ m and r = 9.0 × 10⁶ m, so that ΔEg ≈ 12.5 × 2.5 × 10⁸ ≈ 3.1 × 10⁹ J. But mark well the units of the axes before you multiply: distances are often given in km, and axes often bear scale factors such as ×10⁶.

greekepic.27: An astronaut “floating” aboard the International Space Station has by no means escaped the gravitational field of the Earth. At the ISS altitude of some 400 km, r = 6.77 × 10⁶ m and g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶)² = 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — fully 89% of its worth at the surface.
greekepic.28: The astronaut floats because the station and all that it holds are falling freely together, all alike quickening at 8.7 m s⁻² toward the Earth's centre. cnpop.0: 引力场是我们第一个要拿来精确开刀的场：它有多强、能拽出多大的力、穿过它要存多少能量，还有最离谱的——它能不烧一滴油、不点一台发动机，就把卫星稳稳吊在天上转圈。
cnpop.1: 注意看，每一坨质量身边都裹着一层引力场。某一点的引力场强度 g，说白了就是：你往那个点扔进去每一千克质量，场会拽它多大的力。对于一个质量为 M 的球形天体，在离球心 r 处的场强是：
cnpop.2: 场强是个矢量，方向感很强。在每一个点上它都死死指向源质量的球心，所以一颗行星的场是辐射状的、从四面八方往里收。大小这块儿，它老老实实服从平方反比律：距离翻一倍，场强直接缩成四分之一。
cnpop.3: 把公式纸上地球的数据塞进去（M = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg，r = RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m），就得到 g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.37 × 10⁶)² = 9.81 N kg⁻¹，这是地表的值。跑到离球心 r = 2RE 处，场强就是 9.81 ÷ 4 = 2.45 N kg⁻¹；到 3RE 又变成 9.81 ÷ 9 = 1.09 N kg⁻¹。注意，钻进行星内部这公式就罢工了：场会一路往球心跌到零，所以 g 对 r 的图像在地表那儿会拐出一个尖尖的折角，如图 2.1 所示。
cnpop.4: 场强的两个单位其实是一回事：1 N kg⁻¹ = 1 m s⁻²。场拽一个质量 m 的力是 Fg = mg，所以一个自由下落的物体加速度就是 a = Fg/m = g。看好了——质量直接被约掉！同一个点上自由下落的所有物体，不管几斤几两，加速度都一模一样。
cnpop.5: Key idea 某一点的引力场强度，就是那一点上每千克受到的力——因此也就是任何物体在那儿的自由落体加速度。N kg⁻¹ 和 m s⁻² 是同一个单位，别当成俩。
cnpop.6: Connects AOS 1 里那一堆抛体题、竖直圆周题，用的全是这同一个场：地表附近那个雷打不动的向下加速度 9.8 m s⁻²，本质就是 g = GM/r²，只不过因为高度变化跟 RE 比起来小得可怜，我们就当它是均匀的来用。
cnpop.7: 任意两坨质量都会互相吸引，谁也跑不掉。牛顿的万有引力定律给出了它俩互相拽的那个力的大小：
cnpop.8: 这两个力是一对牛顿第三定律的搭档：地球拽卫星的力，和卫星拽地球的力，大小相等、方向相反，如图 2.2 所示。它俩永远抵消不了，因为它们压根作用在不同的物体上。万有引力定律和场的画面完美对上号：在场强为 g 的那一点，质量 m 受到的力就是 Fg = mg，因为 g = GM/r²。
cnpop.9: Trap 划重点别踩坑：一颗卫星“在 630 km 的高度”，可不等于 r = 6.30 × 10⁵ m。高度 h 是从地表量起的，但 r 是从地球球心量起的：r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m。忘了加 RE，或者把单位是 km 的高度硬塞进单位是 m 的半径里，这是本节翻车率最高的经典操作。
cnpop.10: Lucia 正在追踪一颗 750 kg 的通信卫星，它在 6.30 × 10⁵ m 高度上跑圆轨道。她把卫星当成一个质点来处理，并直接用公式纸上地球的数据。
cnpop.11: 公式：Fg = G m1m2r²，其中 r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m。
cnpop.13: 答案：Fg = 6.09 × 10³ N，方向从卫星指向地球球心。再由牛顿第三定律一甩，卫星也回敬地球一个 6.09 × 10³ N 的力，方向从地球球心指向卫星。
cnpop.14: 把一坨质量抬高、让它穿过引力场，就会存下引力势能。在地表附近，g 基本钉死在 9.81 N kg⁻¹ 不动，这时能量的变化是：
cnpop.15: 只要高度变化跟 RE 比起来小打小闹——扔个球、坐个电梯、飞架飞机——这套均匀场近似就香得不行。可一旦卫星要被抬高好几千千米，它就崩了，因为 g 本身会随高度往下掉。这时候通用的招数，是把 mgΔh 换成一块面积。
cnpop.16: 引力势能的变化，等于力—距离图像下方的面积，取两个距离之间那一块——跟前几个单元里弹簧、小车用的“功就是面积”那条规则一模一样。要是图像画的是场强 g 对距离，那它下方面积的单位就是 (N kg⁻¹) × m = J kg⁻¹：它给出的是每千克的能量变化，得乘上质量 m 才能换成 ΔEg。妙就妙在，场—距离图像跟绕着转的那个物体毫无关系，所以一张图就能通吃各种质量的卫星。
cnpop.17: 估面积的土办法：数格子。先算出一个格子值多少能量——它高度跨过的力，乘上它宽度跨过的距离——然后把每一个至少有一半压在曲线下面的格子都数进去。在图 2.3 里，每个格子代表 500 N × 5.0 × 10⁵ m = 2.5 × 10⁸ J，在 r = 7.0 × 10⁶ m 到 r = 9.0 × 10⁶ m 之间大约有 12.5 个格子躺在曲线下，所以 ΔEg ≈ 12.5 × 2.5 × 10⁸ ≈ 3.1 × 10⁹ J。动手乘之前先盯一眼坐标轴单位：距离常常用 km 给，坐标轴还经常背着 ×10⁶ 这种比例因子，一不留神就翻车。
cnpop.18: 一颗稳定跑圆轨道的卫星，身上有且只有一个力在作用：中心天体拽它的那股引力。没有任何别的力来给它配平。卫星一刻不停地朝中心加速，而它那个切向速度则把它甩着绕圈、而不是直接砸下来。它跑的是恒定速率，但绝不是恒定速度，因为它的运动方向每一瞬间都在变。
cnpop.19: 引力场刚刚好、分毫不差地供上了匀速圆周运动所需的向心加速度。对于半径 r、周期 T 的圆轨道：
cnpop.20: 把场强和向心加速度划上等号，就把轨道和中心质量死死绑在了一起：GM/r² = v²/r = 4π²r/T²。注意看，卫星自己的质量在这条方程里压根没出现。对于一个定下来的中心天体，你一旦选定了轨道半径，速度和周期也就被锁死了——同一半径上，一颗螺丝钉和一座空间站绕得一模一样。
cnpop.21: Key idea 轨道速度、周期和加速度，只看中心质量 M 和轨道半径 r 这两样——跟卫星自己有多重半毛钱关系都没有。
cnpop.22: 地球同步卫星永远悬在赤道上同一个点的正上方，所以地面的接收锅压根不用追着它转。Kenji 把这颗卫星的运动建模成赤道平面内的一个匀速圆轨道，沿着地球自转的方向跑，周期正好是一天，T = 24 h = 8.64 × 10⁴ s。
cnpop.23: 公式：场供上向心加速度，G Mr² = 4π²rT²，整理一下就变成 r³ = GMT²4π²。
cnpop.25: 求值：r = ∛(7.53 × 10²²) = 4.22 × 10⁷ m，于是高度就是 h = r − RE = 4.22 × 10⁷ − 6.37 × 10⁶ = 3.59 × 10⁷ m（大概在地表上方 36 000 km）。
cnpop.26: 答案：轨道速度 v = 2πr/T = (2π × 4.22 × 10⁷) ÷ (8.64 × 10⁴) = 3.07 × 10³ m s⁻¹。每一颗地球同步卫星，不管它多重，都只能挤在这唯一的一个半径上，没得商量。
cnpop.27: 一个在国际空间站里“飘”着的宇航员，可没有逃出地球的引力场。在 ISS 大约 400 km 的高度上，r = 6.77 × 10⁶ m，g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶)² = 8.7 N kg⁻¹——足足是地表值的约 89%，一点都不少。
cnpop.28: 宇航员之所以飘，是因为空间站连同里头的一切都在一起自由下落，全体以 8.7 m s⁻² 朝地球球心加速。地板从来没压过宇航员的脚，所以法向力为零：FN = 0，而 Fg 却大得很。表观失重，指的是法向力没了，可不是引力没了。
cnpop.29: Trap “太空里没有引力”这句话，一句错俩。ISS 高度上的场大约是地表值的 89%，而且要是没了它，根本就压根没有轨道这回事——卫星会沿直线匀速飞走，头也不回。在轨失重的意思是 FN = 0；它从来、绝不意味着 Fg = 0。
cnpop.30: Connects 第四单元还会再回到这些轨道上来：GPS 卫星发的时间信号，因为它们的轨道速度，必须做狭义相对论的修正——所以本页这个圆轨道模型，正是一个能被实打实测出来的相对论效应的起跑线。

ai.0: Here’s the first field you’ll get to treat with real numbers: gravity. We’ll work out how strong it is, the force it pushes with, the energy you bank by climbing through it, and — most beautifully — how it holds whole worlds in orbit without a single engine running.
ai.1: Every mass drapes the space around it in a gravitational field. The gravitational field strength g at a point is simply the gravitational force felt by each kilogram of mass you place there. For a spherical body of mass M, the field strength a distance r from its centre works out to:
ai.2: Field strength is a vector. At every point it aims straight in toward the centre of the source mass, so a planet’s field spreads out like spokes — radial. And its size follows the inverse-square law: step out to twice the distance from the centre and the field drops to a quarter.
ai.3: Drop in the formula-sheet numbers for Earth (M = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg, r = RE = 6.37 × 10⁶ m) and you get g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.37 × 10⁶)² = 9.81 N kg⁻¹ right at the surface. Climb out to r = 2RE from the centre and it’s down to 9.81 ÷ 4 = 2.45 N kg⁻¹; at 3RE it’s just 9.81 ÷ 9 = 1.09 N kg⁻¹. Dig inside the planet, though, and the formula gives up: the field there fades toward zero at the very centre, so the graph of g against r has a sharp kink right at the surface, as shown in Figure 2.1.
ai.4: Those two units for field strength are really the same thing: 1 N kg⁻¹ = 1 m s⁻². The force the field lays on a mass m is Fg = mg, so anything in free fall accelerates at a = Fg/m = g. Watch the mass cancel right out — which is why every object falling freely at the same spot picks up speed at exactly the same rate, no matter how heavy it is.
ai.5: Key idea Gravitational field strength at a point is the force per kilogram — and so the free-fall acceleration of any object — right there. N kg⁻¹ and m s⁻² are one and the same unit.
ai.6: Connects Every projectile and vertical-circle problem back in AOS 1 leans on this very field: that steady downward acceleration of 9.8 m s⁻² near the surface is just g = GM/r² treated as uniform — an approximation you can get away with because the height changes are tiny next to RE.
ai.7: Any two masses pull on each other. Newton’s law of universal gravitation hands you the size of the force each one feels from the other:
ai.8: Those two forces are a Newton’s third law pair: Earth’s pull on the satellite and the satellite’s pull on Earth match in size and point opposite ways, as shown in Figure 2.2. They never cancel, because they act on different objects. The universal law and the field picture tell the same story: the force on a mass m sitting where the field strength is g is Fg = mg, since g = GM/r².
ai.9: Trap A satellite “at an altitude of 630 km” is not sitting at r = 6.30 × 10⁵ m. Altitude h is measured up from the surface, but r is measured from Earth’s centre: r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m. Forgetting to add RE, or tossing an altitude in km onto a radius in m, is the single most common slip in this whole topic.
ai.10: Lucia is tracking a 750 kg communications satellite circling at an altitude of 6.30 × 10⁵ m. She treats the satellite as a point mass and pulls the Earth data straight off the formula sheet.
ai.11: Formula: Fg = G m1m2r² with r = RE + h = 6.37 × 10⁶ + 6.30 × 10⁵ = 7.00 × 10⁶ m.
ai.13: Answer: Fg = 6.09 × 10³ N, pointing from the satellite in toward Earth’s centre. And by Newton’s third law, the satellite tugs back on Earth with a force of 6.09 × 10³ N, directed from Earth’s centre out toward the satellite.

ai.15: For height changes that stay small next to RE — a thrown ball, a lift, an aircraft — this uniform-field shortcut is spot on. But raise a satellite through thousands of kilometres and it falls apart, because g itself thins out with altitude. The general fix swaps mgΔh for an area.
ai.16: The change in gravitational potential energy is just the area under a force–distance graph between the two distances — the very same work-as-area trick you used for springs and trolleys in earlier units. If instead the graph plots field strength g against distance, its area carries units of (N kg⁻¹) × m = J kg⁻¹: now it gives the energy change per kilogram, and you multiply by the mass m to get ΔEg. The beauty of it: a field–distance graph doesn’t care what’s orbiting, so one graph serves satellites of every mass.
ai.17: To estimate an area, count grid squares. First pin down what one square is worth — the force its height spans times the distance its width spans — then count every square that’s at least half-buried under the curve. In Figure 2.3 each square stands for 500 N × 5.0 × 10⁵ m = 2.5 × 10⁸ J, and roughly 12.5 squares sit under the curve between r = 7.0 × 10⁶ m and r = 9.0 × 10⁶ m, so ΔEg ≈ 12.5 × 2.5 × 10⁸ ≈ 3.1 × 10⁹ J. Always eyeball the axis units before you multiply: distances often sneak in as km, and axes often carry scale factors like ×10⁶.
ai.18: A satellite in a steady circular orbit has exactly one force on it: the gravitational pull from the central body. Nothing balances it. The satellite is forever accelerating toward the centre while its sideways velocity sweeps it around rather than straight down. It moves at constant speed but not constant velocity, because its direction keeps changing every single instant.
ai.19: The gravitational field hands over exactly the centripetal acceleration that uniform circular motion demands. For a circular orbit of radius r and period T:
ai.20: Set the field strength equal to the centripetal acceleration and the orbit is locked to the central mass: GM/r² = v²/r = 4π²r/T². Notice the satellite’s own mass is nowhere in that equation. For a given central body, once you pick the orbital radius you’ve fixed the speed and the period — a stray bolt and a space station at the same radius orbit in lockstep.
ai.21: Key idea Orbital speed, period and acceleration ride only on the central mass M and the orbital radius r — never on the mass of the satellite.
ai.22: A geostationary satellite hangs over the same spot on the equator, so the dishes pointing at it never have to move. Kenji models its motion as a uniform circular orbit in the plane of the equator, sweeping the same way Earth spins with a period of exactly one day, T = 24 h = 8.64 × 10⁴ s.
ai.23: Formula: the field supplies the centripetal acceleration, G Mr² = 4π²rT², which rearranges to r³ = GMT²4π².
ai.25: Evaluation: r = ∛(7.53 × 10²²) = 4.22 × 10⁷ m, which puts the altitude at h = r − RE = 4.22 × 10⁷ − 6.37 × 10⁶ = 3.59 × 10⁷ m (around 36 000 km up).
ai.26: Answer: the orbital speed is v = 2πr/T = (2π × 4.22 × 10⁷) ÷ (8.64 × 10⁴) = 3.07 × 10³ m s⁻¹. Every geostationary satellite, whatever it weighs, has to claim this one exact radius.
ai.27: An astronaut “floating” aboard the International Space Station hasn’t escaped Earth’s gravity at all. At the ISS altitude of roughly 400 km, r = 6.77 × 10⁶ m and g = (6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ × 5.97 × 10²⁴) ÷ (6.77 × 10⁶)² = 8.7 N kg⁻¹ — still about 89% of the value down at the surface.
ai.28: The astronaut floats because the station and everything in it are falling together, all accelerating at 8.7 m s⁻² toward Earth’s centre. The floor never pushes up on the astronaut’s feet, so the normal force is zero: FN = 0 even while Fg stays large. Apparent weightlessness is the absence of a normal force, not the absence of gravitational force.
ai.29: Trap “There’s no gravity in space” is wrong twice over. The field at ISS altitude is about 89% of its surface value, and without it there’d be no orbit at all — the satellite would just sail off in a straight line at constant speed. Weightlessness in orbit means FN = 0; it never means Fg = 0.
ai.30: Connects Unit 4 circles back to these orbits: the time signals from GPS satellites have to be corrected for special relativity because of how fast they’re moving, so the plain circular-orbit model on this page is the launch pad for a real, measurable relativistic effect.
